name: Prakashatman
dates_notes: Date heavily contested: traditional 10th c., modern Hacker / Potter 13th c.
core_thesis: Prakāśātman is the system-builder of *Vivaraṇa*-Advaita, the sub-school that takes its name from his *Pañcapādikā-Vivaraṇa* and that defines itself against the *Bhāmatī* on every load-bearing question. His central claim is the *brahmāśrita-avidyā-vāda*: *Brahman* itself, not the *jīva*, is the *āśraya* (locus) of *avidyā*, while *Brahman* is also its *viṣaya* (object) — *avidyā* is *bhāva-rūpā* (positively existent yet *anirvacanīya*), and the *jīva* is *cidābhāsa*, a *pratibimba* of *Brahman* in this beginningless ignorance. From this root he draws (i) the doctrine that the Mahāvākya, qua *śabda-pramāṇa*, generates an *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti* whose function is to remove *avidyā* though knowledge itself is never produced; (ii) a strong rejection of *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* — *karma* purifies but cannot occasion *jñāna*; (iii) the *anirvacanīya-khyāti* analysis of *adhyāsa*. With Sarvajñātman's *Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* upstream and Vidyāraṇya's *Vivaraṇa-Prameya-Saṃgraha* downstream, he supplies the technical vocabulary in which most subsequent Advaita debate is conducted.

engaged_works.title: Pancapadika-Vivarana
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the school-defining text of Vivaraṇa-Advaita.
engaged_works.summary: Massive *vivaraṇa* (gloss-of-a-gloss) on Padmapāda's *Pañcapādikā*, itself a sub-commentary on the opening five *pādas* of Śaṅkara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*. The work converts Padmapāda's compressed positions into a fully articulated metaphysics and epistemology, and in the process founds a sub-school. Its principal contributions: (i) the *brahmāśrita-avidyā-vāda* — *Brahman* is the *āśraya* and *viṣaya* of *avidyā*, sharply contra Vāchaspati's *jīva-locus* analysis; (ii) the *bhāva-rūpā-anirvacanīya-avidyā* doctrine — ignorance is positively existent though indeterminable as either *sat* or *asat*; (iii) the *cidābhāsa* / *pratibimba* analysis of the *jīva*; (iv) the doctrine that the Mahāvākya, qua *śabda-pramāṇa*, produces an *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti* whose role is to dissolve *avidyā*; and (v) a sustained refutation of *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*. The *Vivaraṇa* generates the largest commentarial tradition in later Advaita — the *Vivaraṇa-Prameya-Saṃgraha* of Vidyāraṇya, the *Tātparya-Dīpikā* of Citsukha, the *Tattva-Dīpana* of Akhaṇḍānanda — and is the implicit interlocutor of nearly all post-thirteenth-century Advaita scholasticism.
